---
title: "Lab 11: Staggered Difference-in-Differences"
author: ""
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    number_sections: true
    df_print: paged
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.width = 8,
  fig.height = 5
)
```

# Learning objectives

By the end of this lab, you should be able to:

1. Explain why staggered treatment adoption is more complicated than the 2x2 DiD case.
2. Estimate a two-way fixed effects DiD model and describe why its weighting scheme can be problematic with staggered adoption.
3. Use the Goodman-Bacon decomposition to see which comparisons drive a TWFE estimate.
4. Estimate group-time average treatment effects with a modern staggered-DiD estimator.
5. Interpret cohort-specific and event-time treatment effects in an applied policy setting.
6. Propose a feasible extension of a staggered-DiD replication for a final project.

# Why this lab?

Lab 10 used the cleanest DiD setup: one treated group, one control group, one treatment date, and one pre/post comparison. That framework is useful because the identifying logic is transparent.

Many empirical papers are not that clean. Policies are often adopted in different years by different units. Once treatment timing is staggered:

- there is no single 2x2 comparison,
- some treated units may act as controls for other treated units before they adopt,
- and a naive two-way fixed effects regression can mix together comparisons that do not match the causal question we want to answer.

This lab uses a substantive example from **Cheng and Hoekstra (2013), "Does Strengthening Self-Defense Law Deter Crime or Escalate Violence? Evidence from Castle Doctrine."** The paper studies whether Castle Doctrine laws increase homicide. The data are available in the `bacondecomp` package, which makes the application easy to replicate in class.

Our plan is:

1. inspect the treatment timing;
2. estimate a familiar TWFE model;
3. decompose that TWFE estimate with Goodman-Bacon;
4. estimate modern staggered-DiD effects with `did::att_gt()`;
5. discuss what changes in the interpretation.

# 1. Packages and data

## Packages

```{r packages}
install_if_missing <- function(pkgs) {
  missing <- pkgs[!pkgs %in% installed.packages()[, "Package"]]
  if (length(missing) > 0) install.packages(missing)
}

install_if_missing(c(
  "dplyr", "ggplot2", "broom", "knitr",
  "fixest", "did", "bacondecomp"
))

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(broom)
library(knitr)
library(fixest)
library(did)
library(bacondecomp)

theme_set(theme_minimal())
options(dplyr.summarise.inform = FALSE)
```

## Load the Castle Doctrine data

The `castle` dataset is a state-year panel from Cheng and Hoekstra's application. The outcome we study is `l_homicide`, the log homicide rate.

```{r load-data}
data("castle", package = "bacondecomp")

cohorts <- castle %>%
  group_by(state) %>%
  summarise(
    first_treat = as.numeric(if (any(post == 1)) min(year[post == 1]) else 0),
    ever_treated = first_treat > 0
  )

castle <- castle %>%
  left_join(cohorts, by = "state") %>%
  mutate(
    treated_now = as.integer(first_treat > 0 & year >= first_treat),
    cohort = if_else(
      first_treat == 0,
      "Never treated",
      paste("Treat in", first_treat)
    )
  )

stopifnot(all(castle$treated_now == castle$post))
```

## Dataset overview

```{r overview}
castle %>%
  summarise(
    n_obs = n(),
    n_states = n_distinct(state),
    first_year = min(year),
    last_year = max(year),
    ever_treated_states = n_distinct(state[first_treat > 0]),
    never_treated_states = n_distinct(state[first_treat == 0])
  ) %>%
  kable()
```

```{r cohort-table}
cohort_table <- castle %>%
  distinct(state, first_treat) %>%
  mutate(
    cohort = if_else(first_treat == 0, "Never treated", as.character(first_treat))
  ) %>%
  count(first_treat, cohort, name = "states") %>%
  arrange(first_treat == 0, first_treat) %>%
  mutate(cohort = factor(cohort, levels = cohort)) %>%
  select(cohort, states)

cohort_table %>% kable()
```

There are several treatment cohorts rather than one single adoption year. That is exactly why we need a staggered-DiD framework.

## Visualize adoption timing

```{r cohort-plot}
cohort_table %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = cohort, y = states, fill = cohort)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Treatment timing is staggered across states",
    x = "First treatment year",
    y = "Number of states"
  )
```

```{r cohort-trends}
castle %>%
  group_by(year, cohort) %>%
  summarise(mean_l_homicide = mean(l_homicide)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = mean_l_homicide, color = cohort)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(min(castle$year), max(castle$year), by = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Average log homicide by treatment cohort",
    subtitle = "Each line groups states by the year they first adopt Castle Doctrine laws",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Mean log homicide",
    color = NULL
  )
```

> **Discussion:** Why is a single pre/post comparison no longer enough once different states adopt in 2005, 2006, 2007, 2008, and 2009?

# 2. Start with a TWFE staggered DiD

A natural first pass is the familiar two-way fixed effects regression:

Y_{st} = alpha_s + lambda_t + beta D_{st} + epsilon_{st}

where:

- (Y_{st}) is the log homicide rate in state (s) and year (t),
- (D_{st}) equals 1 once a state is exposed to Castle Doctrine,
- (alpha_s) are state fixed effects,
- (lambda_t) are year fixed effects.

```{r twfe}
twfe_mod <- feols(
  l_homicide ~ treated_now | state + year,
  data = castle,
  cluster = ~state
)

tidy(twfe_mod, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  kable(digits = 3)
```

This coefficient is easy to estimate, but with staggered adoption it is not automatically the clean ATT we want. The reason is that TWFE averages together many implicit 2x2 comparisons.

> **Exercise 1:** The TWFE coefficient is in log points. Is it positive or negative, and what would that imply substantively for homicide after adoption?

# 3. Where does the TWFE estimate come from?

The Goodman-Bacon decomposition breaks the TWFE coefficient into the weighted average of all the 2x2 DiD comparisons inside it.

```{r bacon-decomp}
bacon_parts <- as.data.frame(
  bacon(
    l_homicide ~ treated_now,
    data = castle,
    id_var = "state",
    time_var = "year"
  )
)

bacon_summary <- data.frame(
  type = names(tapply(bacon_parts$weight, bacon_parts$type, sum)),
  weight = as.numeric(tapply(bacon_parts$weight, bacon_parts$type, sum)),
  avg_est = as.numeric(
    tapply(bacon_parts$estimate * bacon_parts$weight, bacon_parts$type, sum) /
      tapply(bacon_parts$weight, bacon_parts$type, sum)
  )
)

bacon_summary %>% kable(digits = 3)
```

```{r bacon-plot}
ggplot(bacon_parts, aes(x = weight, y = estimate, color = type, shape = type)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  labs(
    title = "Goodman-Bacon decomposition of the TWFE estimate",
    subtitle = "Each point is one implicit 2x2 DiD comparison inside the TWFE regression",
    x = "Weight in the TWFE average",
    y = "2x2 DiD estimate",
    color = NULL,
    shape = NULL
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The key lesson is:

- some weight comes from treated vs never-treated comparisons, which are often the cleanest;
- some weight comes from earlier-treated vs later-treated comparisons;
- some weight comes from later-treated vs earlier-treated comparisons.

Those last two categories can be problematic because a treated unit may be used as a control for another treated unit. If treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or over exposure length, the TWFE average can become hard to interpret.

> **Exercise 2:** Suppose treatment effects grow over time after adoption. Why might an "earlier-treated vs later-treated" comparison distort the overall TWFE estimate?

# 4. Estimate modern staggered-DiD effects

To avoid contaminated comparisons, we now estimate group-time average treatment effects using `did::att_gt()`.

Our control group will be **not-yet-treated states**, meaning states that have not adopted Castle Doctrine by a given year. This is closer to the logic of "clean controls" from Lab 10.

```{r att-gt}
cs_mod <- att_gt(
  yname = "l_homicide",
  tname = "year",
  idname = "sid",
  gname = "first_treat",
  data = castle,
  control_group = "nevertreated",
  est_method = "reg",
  bstrap = FALSE,
  cband = FALSE
)

overall_att <- aggte(cs_mod, type = "simple") # overall ATT across all cohorts and time periods
group_att <- aggte(cs_mod, type = "group") # cohort-specific ATTs by first treatment year
dynamic_att <- aggte(cs_mod, type = "dynamic") # event-study aggregation of ATTs by time relative to treatment
```

## Compare TWFE to the modern ATT

```{r compare-table}
comparison <- tibble(
  model = c("TWFE", "Callaway-Sant'Anna overall ATT"),
  estimate = c(
    tidy(twfe_mod)$estimate,
    overall_att$overall.att
  ),
  std.error = c(
    tidy(twfe_mod)$std.error,
    overall_att$overall.se
  )
)

comparison %>% kable(digits = 3)
```

```{r overall-interpretation}
percent_effect <- 100 * (exp(overall_att$overall.att) - 1)
percent_effect
```

The overall staggered-DiD estimate is about `r round(percent_effect, 1)` percent in homicide, which is close to the broad substantive conclusion of Cheng and Hoekstra: Castle Doctrine expansions are associated with higher homicide rather than clear deterrence.

> **Discussion:** Why is the modern staggered-DiD estimate larger than the TWFE estimate in this application? What comparisons are being removed or reweighted?

## Cohort-specific effects

```{r cohort-effects}
group_table <- tibble(
  cohort = group_att$egt,
  estimate = group_att$att.egt,
  std.error = group_att$se.egt,
  lower = estimate - 1.96 * std.error,
  upper = estimate + 1.96 * std.error
)

group_table %>% kable(digits = 3)
```

```{r cohort-effects-plot}
ggplot(group_table, aes(x = factor(cohort), y = estimate)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), width = 0.15) +
  geom_point(size = 2.8, color = "firebrick") +
  labs(
    title = "Cohort-specific staggered-DiD estimates",
    x = "First treatment year",
    y = "ATT on log homicide"
  )
```

The cohort estimates show that most adoption cohorts from 2005 to 2008 have positive effects, while the 2009 cohort is much less precise because there is very little post-treatment time left in the sample.

## Event-time dynamics

```{r dynamic-plot}
ggdid(dynamic_att) +
  labs(
    title = "Event-study aggregation from the staggered-DiD model",
    x = "Event time",
    y = "ATT on log homicide"
  )
```

This dynamic plot should be read carefully:

- event time 0 is the treatment year,
- positive post-treatment effects suggest homicide rises after adoption,
- very early leads or very late lags are based on fewer cohorts and should not be overinterpreted.

> **Exercise 3:** Which event times look most informative here? Which ones are likely to be noisy because only a few cohorts contribute to them?

# 5. Final takeaway

The main message from this lab is that **staggered treatment timing changes the estimator, not just the dataset**.

- In a 2x2 DiD, the ATT is built from one transparent comparison.
- In a staggered design, TWFE averages many comparisons together.
- The Goodman-Bacon decomposition helps us see those hidden comparisons.
- Modern estimators such as Callaway-Sant'Anna recover group-time ATTs using cleaner control groups.

That is why the applied workflow for staggered DiD should usually be:

1. map the treatment timing;
2. estimate the familiar TWFE model as a benchmark;
3. inspect where that benchmark gets its identifying variation;
4. report a modern staggered-DiD estimate that matches the causal question more closely.
